- **Elevations:** straight-on views of each side of the building.
- **Sections:** cutaway views showing how parts are built inside.
- **Detail drawings:** close-ups of specific connections or features.

Blueprints are drawn to scale, meaning a small measurement on paper equals a larger real-world distance. A common scale is 1/4 inch equals 1 foot. A scale ruler helps you read these measurements, though written dimensions on the drawing are always the most accurate, so use them first.

Plans use standard symbols and abbreviations for things like doors, windows, electrical outlets, and plumbing fixtures. A legend on the drawings explains the symbols used. Dimension lines with numbers tell you exact sizes and spacing.

When reading a plan, start with the big picture, then zoom into details. Always check the title block in the corner, which lists the project, the drawing name, the scale, and the revision date. Working from the latest revision is critical, since building from an outdated plan wastes time and material. With practice, reading blueprints becomes second nature and makes you a more skilled, valuable worker.

lessons.title: Equipment Safety
lessons.content:
Construction relies on powerful tools and machines that make work faster but can cause serious injury if misused. Equipment safety is about respecting that power and following proven procedures.

The first rule is simple: never operate equipment you are not trained and authorized to use. Many machines, like forklifts, aerial lifts, and cranes, legally require special certification. Using them without training is dangerous and against the rules.

Before using any tool or machine, do these checks:

- Inspect it for damage, like frayed cords, cracked housings, or worn parts.
- Make sure all guards and safety features are in place. Never remove a guard.
- Check that you have the right PPE for the task.
- Ensure the area is clear of people and obstacles.

While operating, stay focused and follow the manufacturer's instructions. Keep hands and clothing away from moving parts, and never bypass safety devices to work faster. For powered machinery, follow lockout/tagout procedures during maintenance, which means shutting off and locking the energy source so the machine cannot start while you work on it.

Ladders and scaffolds deserve special care, since falls are the top cause of construction deaths. Set ladders on firm ground at the correct angle, maintain three points of contact, and never overreach. Make sure scaffolds are built and inspected by qualified people before you climb.

When equipment problems arise, tag it out of service and report it. Working safely with equipment protects you and everyone around you, and it is a habit that defines a true professional.

questions.prompt: Which PPE protects your head from falling objects on a job site?
questions.explanation: A hard hat protects your head from falling objects and bumps.

questions.prompt: In the hierarchy of controls, where does PPE rank?
options: It is the first and best solution
It is the last line of defense, used after reducing hazards
It replaces all other safety measures
questions.explanation: PPE is the last line of defense; eliminating or reducing a hazard always comes first.

questions.prompt: Which of these is one of OSHA's construction 'Fatal Four' hazards?
questions.explanation: Falls are one of the Fatal Four and the leading cause of construction deaths.

questions.prompt: What is the correct way to lift a heavy object manually?
options: Bend your back and lift fast
Bend your knees, keep your back straight, and lift with your legs
Hold the load away from your body
questions.explanation: Bending the knees, keeping the back straight, and lifting with the legs protects against strains.

questions.prompt: On a blueprint, what does a scale of '1/4 inch equals 1 foot' mean?
options: Each 1/4 inch on paper represents 1 foot in real life
questions.explanation: A scale means a small measurement on paper stands for a larger real-world distance.

questions.prompt: Before operating equipment like a forklift, you must:
options: Just figure it out as you go
Be trained and authorized, often with certification
questions.explanation: Equipment like forklifts requires proper training and certification before you operate it.

questions.prompt: What does lockout/tagout do?
options: Locks off the energy source so a machine cannot start during maintenance
questions.explanation: Lockout/tagout shuts off and locks the energy source so equipment cannot start while being serviced.

questions.prompt: When you spot a hazard you cannot safely fix yourself, you should:
options: Assume someone else will handle it
Mark or barricade it and report it to your supervisor
questions.explanation: Marking or barricading a hazard and reporting it protects everyone on the site.
